diagrams.title: IPv4 Packet Header Structure
diagrams.description: A detailed box diagram showing the 20-byte IPv4 header with its fields: Version (4 bits), IHL (4 bits), Type of Service (8 bits), Total Length (16 bits), Identification (16 bits), Flags (3 bits), Fragment Offset (13 bits), TTL (8 bits), Protocol (8 bits), Header Checksum (16 bits), Source Address (32 bits), Destination Address (32 bits), and Options (variable).
elements: Header field boxes with bit lengths displayed
Row labels and bit position markers
diagrams.purpose: Explains the structure and components of the IPv4 packet header, helping students understand how data is encapsulated and what control information is included in every IPv4 packet.

diagrams.title: IPv6 Packet Header Structure
diagrams.description: A detailed box diagram showing the simplified 40-byte IPv6 header with its fields: Version (4 bits), Traffic Class (8 bits), Flow Label (20 bits), Payload Length (16 bits), Next Header (8 bits), Hop Limit (8 bits), Source Address (128 bits), and Destination Address (128 bits). The design emphasizes the streamlined, fixed-length header compared to IPv4.
elements: Header field boxes with bit lengths displayed
Extension Headers section (shown below main header)
diagrams.purpose: Explains the simplified and optimized IPv6 header structure, highlighting the differences from IPv4 and how the removal of certain fields improves routing efficiency.

diagrams.title: IPv4 vs IPv6 Address Format Comparison
diagrams.description: A side-by-side visual comparison showing IPv4 32-bit address (dotted-decimal notation example: 192.168.1.1) versus IPv6 128-bit address (hexadecimal colon notation example: 2001:0db8:85a3:0000:0000:8a2e:0370:7334). Shows address space size difference with visual representations of bit groups.
elements: 4 groups of 8 bits labeled for IPv4
8 groups of 16 bits labeled for IPv6
Dotted decimal vs hexadecimal notation labels
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates the fundamental difference between IPv4 and IPv6 address formats, showing why IPv6 was introduced to solve the address exhaustion problem and how the notation differs between protocols.
